items.myth: The test set is just a second validation set you can reuse freely.
items.reality: Validation is for repeated experimentation; the test set is a locked final exam. Reusing test metrics for tuning contaminates the estimate — treat test evaluation as rare and pre-registered.
items.example: A team runs 40 architecture trials on test accuracy — reported 0.91 test score, but a fresh hold-out from next month scores 0.74 because the test set was overfit through selection.

items.myth: A bigger training set always beats having a validation split.
items.reality: Without validation, you cannot compare models honestly during development — you either guess hyperparameters or secretly peek at test data. Validation (or CV) is the feedback channel that makes training-set expansion worthwhile.
items.example: Using 100% of data for training with no validation and picking the model that happened to converge last — no principled way to know if depth=4 or depth=12 generalizes better.

items.myth: 70/15/15 is the correct split ratio for every problem.
items.reality: Ratios depend on dataset size and task. Large datasets can use tiny validation/test fractions; small datasets may need cross-validation with a modest locked test slice instead of large fixed hold-outs.
items.example: 1M-row ad click dataset: 98/1/1 still leaves 10k validation and 10k test rows — plenty for stable metrics.

items.myth: If train and test come from the same CSV file, a random split is always valid.
items.reality: Same file does not guarantee i.i.d. rows — temporal order, duplicate users, and near-duplicate records can invalidate random splits. The split strategy must match how unseen production data arrives.
items.example: E-commerce logs with 5 visits per user shuffled randomly — test AUC 0.88; user-level hold-out test AUC 0.71, revealing leakage in the random split.
